sentences.text: No.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When that happens-- actually, I would like to go back a little bit.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember it quite rightly.
When I was on the border with the Russia, in that little town, Korzec--
That is on the beginning of the war.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I notice that the army, the Russian army, and especially the one what were in the big trucks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there have been colossal posters were showing from one side, we know-- and that was a known fact-- that Russia was with Germany, a written-- have got a written [NON-ENGLISH].
I mentioned that to you before.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] between Ribbentrop and that Molotov.
And when I showed the trucks, the big trucks that were some big posters-- were SS, German, with their [NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE], blood's running from his mouth.
And a word here, keeping the standard.
And with the cycle-- cycle and helmet, and this.
I couldn't combine the two things.
On one side is written [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [NON-ENGLISH] between the two countries, between Russia and, and Germany.
And here, again, she poses like this.
I couldn't understand this.
But I came to-- so actually, I was thinking of this, that something is fishy in there.
Something is-- will happen.
And the question, what you gave me now, is telling me that I was right.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That I was thinking about this, but I couldn't believe it.
I couldn't believe it, really.
They came into Vilna through Klaipeda.
That was a port.
And that happens on the 20-- no, when did they?
They invaded Lithuania on the 23rd, 23rd, of June.
And they came in about two days later.
They came into Vilna already.
I remember this quite rightly.
I didn't want, for instance, to work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was on a Monday.
There were two lieutenants, one of the name Habik.
And the other's name was Lavatchek.
And they were assigned [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] as the people [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] from the [INAUDIBLE] east.
And they-- that's in [INAUDIBLE], was in Riga.
And they ask everything was [INAUDIBLE].
They [NON-ENGLISH].
They took over.
That combined.
The [INAUDIBLE] that combined the [SPEAKING GERMAN] in Vilna.
And I didn't went to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They ask-- that was Habik or Lavatchek-- the people who was the technical manager here?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they find out that a gentleman, Kaufman-- was-- Before you go on, could you just spell their names for me please?
Habik-- H-A-B-I-K-- Habik.
And the other's was Lavatchek-- L-A-V-A-T-C-H-E-K.
I beg you?
They was in the-- in the general Wehrmacht.
Wehrmact, the Wehrmacht took over.
Actually, the Wehrmacht took over that combine.
I was.
And they're sending under officer.
And the name, because he was-- I was with-- and the name was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Martin Hanze.
M-A-R-T-I-N, Martin, the first name.
Hanze-- H-A-N-Z-E.
Hanze.
Hanze.
That was his name.
He came.
They asked-- they find out that-- Habik find out where I'm living from the people there in the factory.
He came to me.
Now, I will say this in German, yeah?
Came in.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
I said, yes.
I am Kaufman.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
I said, to him, yes, I was the one.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
I accent this, Herr Kaufman.
Why?
Mister.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SPEAKING GERMAN] And I went to him.
Be so good, Mister Kaufman.
The lieutenant, Habik and Lieutenant Lavatchek want to talk to you.
And I went, and the same thing, that Habik said to me.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
We took over.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That the army took over that battery-- the-- no, the--
They combine the factories.
That what I can't forget too, that what they told me.
[SPEAKING GERMAN]
You have to do this, Mr.
Kaufman.
You have to do this further, to do in the capacity as a technical manager.
And he add to it that [GERMAN], that I will have to eat.
[GERMAN]-- you know?
Provisions?
Provisions.
That I will-- [SPEAKING GERMAN] That there will be for me.
And I was carrying on for them so as I was carrying on for the army, for the Red Army as a technical manager.
Because that combine, when there was a national list, that all products was going to the Red Army.
So and that-- and this-- and I start to work for the-- for the-- for the-- for, for the German army [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] as a technical manager.
The factor-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because that first, when the-- when they-- when they asked me to do that, already Lithuanians were taking over positions.
You know?
There was one engineer, [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- they nominate he was the technical manager.
But he hasn't got the slightest idea about tanning, about carrying on a tannery.
So de facto, I was the one waiting.
Before [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] starts straight away from the beginning, a horrible time.
That was a hell, a horrible time for the Jews.
And that wasn't done-- that was still before the Gestapo or the SS or the SD came to the town.
The Lithuanians, they want-- they called them-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] white arm, arm [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bands.
They-- they call-- they were the Nationalists.
They were the killers.
In the beginning there were some uniforms, too, from the police, from the army, from the ghetto army.
But most of them, they were in civil, carrying white, white bands.
Arm bands.
I can't remember.
I, I left-- I believe-- I don't remember that quite rightly.
I can't remember that quite rightly.
Straight away, killings of Jews.
They were-- they were killing.
They were assembling them in the [NON-ENGLISH], in the [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] They were the one before the [GERMAN] came to Vilna.
And they were the one.
They were the killers.
They were the one.
They were-- they called them harpoonists.
And they were the one, that they were doing the-- all, all, all killings of the Jews.
Killings?
Yes.
When the ghetto-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but that would-- most probably, I will tell you.
[PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] Instead to go to the ghetto, because the ghetto was full-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] so the unlucky people, they were herded in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] jails.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were horrible things there done, really.
I witness, for instance, in the cell, where [INAUDIBLE] 30 or 40 people, there was about 200 or 250 people there.
And one lady, elderly lady, took her life by taking some [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] drugs.
I don't know what that was.
Because you ask me if I was close to it.
So when they start to knock through the door that a person is dying, and so on, they came in.
That was that [GERMAN].
And they said like this-- now [SPEAKING GERMAN] And he took out from-- a revolver and give it [INAUDIBLE] and kill her.
This was the lady who took the--
And that was about-- that was about five meters.
Because everyone was sitting on [INAUDIBLE] and so on.
And I was-- I was a witness to that, what happened that time.
That was on the sixth.
Was it?
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Don't remember the date.
The date when the ghetto was established.
And instead to go to the ghetto-- and that Hanze, that Martin Hanze, he was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] went, also, to the police what was driving us to the ghetto.
Remember to the ghetto, remember to the ghetto.
And the next day, when I didn't turn, that was on a Saturday.
This was on a Saturday.
Yes, and Monday, I didn't came to work.
So they went to look after me in the ghetto.
In the ghetto they find out that I am not there, that he most-- that I am most probably in the in, in [INAUDIBLE] in that--
In that jail.
They was looking for me.
He told me after, when he find me, that was the last cell.
That was a colossal jail.
That he look after me.
Kaufman!
He was calling, Kaufman!
Kaufman!
And I said, here!
[GERMAN] And I took my wife.
And I took, still, Mr.
Fabykant, that he survive, and his-- because he was working also, on the factory.
Fabykant.
F-A-B-Y-K-A-N-T.
Fabykant.
Took them.
And the-- the brother was also trying-- also-- the brother, [?
Moishe ?] Fabykant.
He was also with his wife.
And the daughter.
Fabykant, the wife, and the daughter was-- I took them with me.
You know?
And the door was locked up.
And the brother with the wife and family stayed.
A few hours later, the-- all people there went to Ponary.
They were killed there.
That was the place where the armbands, the Lithuanians, tens of thousands of people in Lithuania were killed through them.
And in Vilna, every second day, every three days, 200, 250, 300 people, Ponary.
They were the killers.
Before, the-- the original Gestapos and SD came into, to, to Vilna, they were the killers.
They were the organizers.
They were the killers.
They were our killers.
Took me out.
And he told us, told my wife and me, [INAUDIBLE], that was the last cell, that he was looking after us.
He engaged two Lithuanians, that all our packs were loaded on that two-wheel, little-- what do you call it?
Cart?
Cart.
And he was pressed.
He brought us in, in, in, into the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the Lithuanians were the one-- the two, it took two Lithuanians to bring it.
It was horrible.
It was a horrible thing there.
Because it was full.
It was closed.
It was a lot of people in there.
To describe, what I would describe, in a room like this one here, that was 16 people.
So at night we were living like sardines, on the-- on the-- on the floor.
The [INAUDIBLE], there's a part of the Jewish quarters have been-- but it wasn't enough for so many people living.

Don't forget, that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Vilna and all little towns around, little villages, little towns, it's over, over, I believe, 90,000 100,000 [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people there, living there.
And this small place was only allocated for that ghetto.
I was spending the year till, till-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] it was September, I believe.
In the tannery.
I was working in tannery.
And some people were working in the tannery, too.
I was.
Yes, I was-- as-- the factory as a technical manager in the tannery for the Wehrmacht.
The tannery was not.
It was outside.
It was-- all tanneries that have been on the-- nearly on the river Vilnia, lying one after the other.
And they make from them, one big combine.
From the ghetto to the tannery, we were going in so-called [NON-ENGLISH].
People not singly, but in parties.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And coming back, also, in [NON-ENGLISH].
Some were guarded through the-- through the Jewish police, there, what was builded, was formed there.
Some through Germans that need them for their [GERMAN].
They need the work done for them.
So a German was cutting.
And we were going without any-- without any-- what do they call it?
Guard.
In the beginning-- in the beginning no.
In the beginning, no.
In march to the tannery, no.
There wasn't-- there wasn't that.
In the ghetto?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Having the contact with the outside, or we were buying the food and trying to bring it in, in ghetto.
But before going in, before the [NON-ENGLISH], you know, before the entrance to the ghetto, always we were-- horrible things were happening.
You know?
Because some Lithuanians from the police and so on, they were looking for some food in everyone's-- so I myself, I got ones from one young German on my face.
So I-- horrible.
In fact, I got some bread brought.
You find some bread and you know?
I escaped that time.
What do you mean, you escaped?
I escaped inside.
You know?
That was so quickly.
He hit me once.
He hit me twice.
And after that--
Yes.
There was a Judenrat.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't remember the names quite rightly.
You know?
I don't remember the names quite rightly.
And after the head of the ghetto, that was [?
Gentz. ?] He was a captain and a Jew, a captain in the-- in the Lithuanian army.
[SIGHS] There was another one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The police was carrying on with the-- there was-- Judenrat was in the beginning-- so-- in beginning.
And after, the police took over.
And that [?
Gentz ?] took over with other, other commandants.
I beg you?
Oh, yes.
I was in the ghetto number one.
Selections, they had been in the number one, and the number one through.
And sending, then, we went to number two.
And after, to Ponary.
Selections.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Something they find out, some other things, the big selections.
You know?
The fight?
That happens every-- no.
We didn't know about this.
But it happens.
So we know about this, that it's going to make selections.
They want whatever's there, right and [INAUDIBLE] or-- and one went to Ponary.
The others went to work.
First-- no.
No, I wouldn't say that.
I was actually-- it's very-- I got a schein.
So long as I was in the ghetto.
A schein, that is papers that I'm working for the-- in the tanneries.
And, you know, that the products produced there, they are very essential in time for the war.

So they were-- they were trying to have the people that they will-- It's hard-- For instance, somebody telled to-- tell me, all right, the Germans did out a good-- a good job because they were trying to give you work and to, to, to have this supply, you know, of-- to, to feed you and so on.
So from the other side, I'm thinking that I believe that they were hit-- watching themselves, not to be sent to the East Front.
You know?
And better to be occupied.
And they were looking for themself, I believe, more than for the-- for the Jews.
No one was good to us.
So you had the schein, the work permit, which allowed you to--
To actually-- that I was not selected to go to Ponary.
I'm talking about the times in Vilna ghetto.
Working also, in the tannery.
Going in the [NON-ENGLISH], and going to work to-- so decide to work in the tanneries.
Good.
